title: practical english usage - Part 71
content:
4 can/could         always Do/Can you play the piano?

She speaks Greek. / She can speak Greek.

or to play instruments.

We   often leave out can when we  are talking about the ability to speak languages 3  languages        and   instruments:        She    speaks        Greek.

I'm so glad that you could come.

Could can also sometimes refer to one occasion in subordinate clauses.

She could hardly believe her eyes. I could only get six eggs.

that have a negative sense.

And we  can use could to talk about one occasion with words like hardly or only, I could understand everything she said.

I could smell something burning.

smell, understand, remember and guess (►  84).

However, could can refer to one occasion with certain verbs: see, hear, taste, feel, coat-yesterday.) not I managed to buy a really nice coat yesterday, (     4-eeuld-buy-a-reaUy-niee in the past (►  82.4).

Could is not normally used to say that somebody did something on one occasion 2 could      in  the   past possible to go...) or One day people will be able to go to the moon for the weekend, (   it will be not (     Icrut-speakFrench-...) I'll be able to speak French at the end of this course.

In other cases we  prefer other structures, for example will be able to.

Can you come to a party on Saturday?

I haven't got time today, but I can see you tomorrow.

I’ve bought the tent, so we can go camping next weekend if we want to.

She can win the race tomorrow if she really tries.

present ability, present circumstances, present decisions, etc.

We   use can to talk about future actions which  we  will be able to do because of 1 future:    can    or  will    be   able 83 a bility:   advanced       points Section 7 Modal    Auxiliary      Verbs grammar •  84  can and could with see, hear, etc For differences between could and was able to, ►    82.4, 83.2.

He can't be understood. (More natural than He's not able to be understood.) Able is not often followed by passive infinitives.

1 might be able to help you. (     I might can .. .) not What have you been able to find out? (     What have you -could •.. . ?) One day scientists will be able to find a cure for cancer.

grammatically possible because it has no infinitive or participles (►  68.1).

Be able to is used in cases (e.g. future, present perfect) where can/could is not tonnes.) This crane can lift 30 tonnes. (More natural than This crane is able to lift 30 Jake can lift / is able to lift 150 kilos.

Be able to is used most often for people or other living things. Compare: I can see a ship. (More natural than I am able to see a ship.) Can you knit? (More natural than Are you able to knit?) can hear, etc (►  84).

Can is preferred in the sense of 'know how  to', and in expressions like can see, I am unable to / can't understand what she wants.

Some people are able to / can walk on their hands.

same meaning as can (►  82). There is a negative form unable.

We   use able especially in the structure be able to + infinitive. This often has the 85  be   able     to 1 (can) remember your grandfather.

Do/Can you follow what he's saying?

I can't/don't understand what she's talking about.

to the meaning.

Can/could is often used with these verbs too. It does not always add very much 3  understand,            follow,       remember not You can tell he's Irish from his accent, (     Yett-c-an-knew-. ..) / could guess what she wanted.

Can/could are not normally used with know in the sense of'find out' (►   504.5).

Can and could are often used with guess and with tell (meaning see, know).

In American English, I see/hear, etc are common in this sense.

Suddenly she realised she could smell something burning.

What did you put in the stew? I can taste something funny.

Can you hear somebody coming up the stairs?

I can see Susan coming, (     I'm seeing . ..) (especially in British English).

hearing, etc at a particular moment, we  often use can see, can hear, etc ears, etc), we  do not normally use progressive forms. To  talk about seeing, When  these verbs refer to perception (receiving information through the eyes, 1  see,   hear,     feel,    smell,      taste 84 can    and   could      with    see,    hear,     etc grammar •  86  typical behaviour: can, could, may, might, will, would You WOULD  tell Emily about the party - I didn't want to invite her.
